Quote: You are what your deep, driving desire is. As your desire is, so is your will. As your will is, so is your deed. As your deed is, so is your destiny.
A man is made by his belief. As he believes, so he is.
The mind is everything. What you think you become.
Lift yourself by yourself; do not degrade yourself. For the self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
When mindfulness is established, the mind does not wander to the past or future; it stays in the present.
He who sees all beings in his own self, and his own self in all beings, loses all fear.
Action is greater than inaction. Perform therefore your task in life. Even the life of the body could not be if there were no action.
Truth is one, sages call it by various names.
Knowledge is the ultimate wealth, for it cannot be stolen, neither diminished by giving.
The soul is never born and never dies; it is eternal, indestructible, and immutable.
In the depths of meditation, one reaches a state of supreme peace and realizes the eternal truth.
Do your work with the welfare of others always in mind.
Be the witness of your thoughts, not the judge. Focus on the light of your own consciousness.
Let your mind be free from attachment and aversion, and you will find inner peace.
That which is the subtle essence, in it all that exists has its self. It is the True. It is the Self, and thou, O Svetaketu, art it.
He who has overcome his desires and found peace within, is free from all sorrow.
The wise see the same presence in a humble scholar, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and an outcaste.
As the rivers flowing east and west merge in the sea and become one with it, forgetting they were separate rivers, so do all creatures lose their separateness when they merge at last into pure Being.
To the unillumined, the body is the self. To the illumined, the Self is the body.
There is no purifier in this world like knowledge. He who is perfected in yoga finds it in himself in due time.
By the grace of the divine, the mind finds rest in the supreme self.
One should not rejoice when obtaining what is pleasant, nor grieve when obtaining what is unpleasant.
The self cannot be attained by the weak, nor by the careless, nor by aimless disciplines.
He who perceives the supreme lord abiding equally in all beings, not dying when they die, he truly sees.
May we hear auspicious words with our ears, may we see auspicious things with our eyes.
Let noble thoughts come to us from every side.
The mind is restless, turbulent, obstinate and very strong, O Krishna, and to subdue it, I think, is more difficult than controlling the wind.
The unreal has no existence, and the real never ceases to be; the truth about both has been realized by the seers.
Yoga is the cessation of the movements of the mind.

Those who are free from anger and all material desires, who are self-realized, self-disciplined and constantly endeavoring for perfection, are assured of liberation in the Supreme in the very near future.
As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones.
From the unreal lead me to the real, from darkness lead me to light, from death lead me to immortality.
We are like the spider. We weave our life and then move along in it. We are like the dreamer who dreams and then lives in the dream.
Through the practice of yoga, the mind becomes still, and the practitioner perceives the self by the self, and rejoices in the self.
He who seeks truth shall find it, and in finding it, he finds all that is worth finding.
Sever the ignorant doubt in your heart with the sword of self-knowledge. Observe your discipline. Arise.
Those whose minds are established in sameness have already conquered the conditions of birth and death.
There is nothing higher than the Truth.
Like two birds of golden plumage, inseparable companions, the individual self and the immortal Self are perched on the branches of the selfsame tree.
All that is visible is an illusion; the unseen alone is real.
Just as a fire is covered by smoke, a mirror by dust, and an embryo by the womb, similarly, the living entity is covered by different degrees of this lust.
Focus on your duty, but do not be attached to the fruits of your actions.
The self is hidden in the hearts of all. By the grace of the Creator, he who is free from sorrow beholds the majesty of the self.
Arise, awake, and stop not till the goal is reached.
It is better to live your own destiny imperfectly than to live an imitation of somebody else's life with perfection.
The peace of God is with them whose mind and soul are in harmony, who are free from desire and wrath, who know their own soul.
Even as the sun shines, illuminating all space, so does the Lord illumine all that exists.
Those who are constantly devoted to me with love, I give them the understanding by which they can come to me.
A gift is pure when it is given from the heart to the right person at the right time and at the right place, and when we expect nothing in return.

Happiness which comes from long practice, which leads to the end of suffering, which at first is like poison, but at last like nectar - this kind of happiness arises from the serenity of one's own mind.
Perform your duty equably, O Arjuna, abandoning all attachment to success or failure. Such equanimity is called Yoga.
Set thy heart upon thy work, but never on its reward.
There is neither this world nor the world beyond nor happiness for the one who doubts.
No one who does good work will ever come to a bad end, either here or in the world to come.
Curving back within myself I create again and again.
The self-controlled soul, who moves amongst sense objects, free from either attachment or repulsion, he wins eternal Peace.
The power of God is with you at all times; through the activities of mind, senses, breathing, and emotions.
Whatever happened, happened for the good. Whatever is happening, is happening for the good. Whatever will happen, will also happen for the good.
Reshape yourself through the power of your will; never let yourself be degraded by self-will.
Hell has three gates: lust, anger, and greed.
As the heat of a fire reduces wood to ashes, the fire of knowledge burns to ashes all karma.
A spiritually situated person is steadfast and undisturbed in both happiness and distress.
Out of compassion for them, I, dwelling in their hearts, destroy with the shining lamp of knowledge the darkness born of ignorance.
By knowing the Supreme, one goes beyond the sorrows of life.
To act is yours, but not to the fruits of your actions.
That pure consciousness, which permeates all beings, is the ultimate reality.
May all beings be happy. May all beings be healthy. May all beings experience prosperity. May none in the world suffer.
He whose mind is steady, whose senses are controlled, who is free from desire, he is a true yogi.
For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
Seek refuge in the attitude of detachment and you will amass the wealth of spiritual awareness.
The self is endless, beginningless, and boundless. Therefore, be not attached to anything.
The highest devotion is that which does not seek a reward, but finds its joy in the Divine alone.
True strength lies in self-restraint and forgiveness.
Patience is the foundation of peace.
Compassion is the root of all holy practices.
By serving others, one purifies the self.
The Lord is the friend of all beings; he who acts with this knowledge never falters.
Do not let your mind be disturbed by the passing storms of the world; dive deep into the ocean of inner calm.
The wise consider both praise and blame as the same, knowing them to be transient.
Joy and sorrow, success and failure, are like the turning of a wheel; do not be overly attached to either.
The light of consciousness is the true illuminating force in the universe.
Recognize the divine in all forms, from the smallest insect to the greatest sage.
He who lives in harmony with himself lives in harmony with the universe.
Let your speech be true, pleasant, and beneficial.
Non-violence in thought, word, and deed is the highest dharma.
Just as rivers merge into the ocean, all paths sincerely followed lead to the Supreme.
The realization of the self is the highest goal of human existence.
Meditation brings wisdom; lack of meditation leaves ignorance. Know well what leads you forward and what holds you back.
He who has faith attains knowledge.
Doubt is the destroyer of peace and the enemy of spiritual progress.
When the mind is purified by selfless action, one effortlessly attains spiritual wisdom.
The Divine is not far away; it pervades the entire universe and dwells within your own heart.
By conquering the ego, one conquers the whole world.
The true warrior is one who fights the battles of the mind and emerges victorious.
Do not grieve over what is lost; rather, focus on realizing the imperishable self.
Wisdom is a boat that carries you across the turbulent ocean of existence.
One who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is intelligent among men.
Attachment is the root cause of suffering. Cut it with the axe of detachment.
In the calmness of the mind, one finds the reflection of the Divine.
The wise man lets go of all results, whether good or bad, and focuses only on the action itself.
True freedom is freedom from the dictates of the wandering mind.
He who offers to me with devotion a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or even water, I accept that offering from the pure of heart.
Live in the world, but let not the world live in you.
A mind free from all disturbances is the clearest mirror of reality.
Do not be proud of wealth, people, or youth; time takes them all away in a minute.
Realize the self always to be neither the body nor the mind, but the witnessing consciousness.
Lust, anger, and greed are the three gates to self-destruction. Abandon them.
The senses are higher than the body, the mind higher than the senses, the intellect higher than the mind, and the soul is the highest of all.
Through deep meditation, one attains that supreme state where words return along with the mind, not finding it.
God dwells in all beings. Do not fail to recognize Him by limiting Him to a particular form.
The entire universe is established in the absolute truth.
May we lead a life of harmony and continuous progress.
O mind, be a bee drinking the nectar of the lotus feet of the Divine.
As is the seed, so is the tree; as is the karma, so is the fortune.
By truthfulness alone is the ultimate reality attained.
Let the mind be absorbed in contemplation of that which is beyond all manifestations.
The ignorant work for their own profit, the wise work for the welfare of the world.
Fearlessness is the first requisite of spirituality. Cowards can never be moral.
One who is always steadfast in his devotion remains unperturbed in any circumstance.
Every action must be a sacred offering to the Supreme.
The true essence of religion is the realization of God.
Let your heart be a sanctuary of pure love and your mind a temple of deep silence.
Through the practice of selfless service, the heart is purified and the ego is dissolved.
You alone are the master of your destiny, the author of your own story.
Knowledge that brings humility is true knowledge.
Forgiveness is the supreme virtue.
Never cause suffering to any living being.
Truthfulness is the foundation of all righteous living.
The divine is the thread on which all pearls of the universe are strung.
When the senses are controlled, the mind is steady, and the intellect is sharp, the light of the self shines forth.
He who looks at all beings equally is truly wise.
There is no wealth like knowledge, no poverty like ignorance.
The mind is its own place, and in itself can make a heaven of hell, a hell of heaven.
Be not sorrowful, for the essence of your being is pure joy.
The one who is free from expectation and egoism achieves supreme peace.
True happiness is found within, not in external objects.
The light of the self dispels the darkness of ignorance.
The universe is an expression of divine joy.
Let us unitedly work together, let us speak together, let our minds understand together.
May our study be vigorous and fruitful, may we not hate each other.
Lead me from falsehood to truth, from darkness to light.
The ultimate reality is formless, yet it assumes forms for the sake of the devotee.
He who performs his bounden duty without depending on the fruits of his action is a yogi.
Devotion is the easiest path to reach the Divine.
Love of God is the supreme goal of life.
The one who serves the devotees of God is the greatest of all devotees.
God sees the heart, not the outward forms of worship.
The name of the Lord is the most powerful purifier in this age.
Surrender entirely to Him with all your heart. By His grace, you will attain supreme peace.
Only the mind that is grounded in tranquility can reflect the divine.
A disciplined mind acts as the best friend; an undisciplined one acts as the worst enemy.
As an ocean remains calm despite rivers flowing into it, so does a wise person remain undisturbed among desires.
Wisdom, discipline, and steadfastness are the ornaments of a true seeker.
Renunciation is not giving up action, but giving up the attachment to the results of action.
The supreme peace is achieved when one realizes the oneness of all existence.
The self is not born, it does not die; it has not sprung from anything; nothing has sprung from it.
It is the self alone that is to be heard of, reflected on, and meditated upon.
To know that one is the witness of the mind is to be free.
The mind, when directed towards worldly objects, causes bondage; when directed towards the divine, it brings liberation.
By knowing the one pure consciousness, all is known.
That which cannot be spoken by speech, but by which speech is spoken—know that alone to be the Divine.
That which cannot be seen by the eye, but by which the eye is able to see—know that alone to be the Divine.
That which cannot be thought by the mind, but by which the mind is able to think—know that alone to be Divine.
The absolute reality is truth, knowledge, and infinity.
Through deep concentration and self-control, one can transcend the limits of the physical body.
Austerity of speech consists in speaking words that are truthful, pleasing, beneficial, and not agitating to others.
Austerity of the mind consists in satisfaction, simplicity, gravity, self-control, and purification of one's existence.
Those who are disciplined in eating, resting, working, and recreating can mitigate all material pains by practicing yoga.
Always remember the Divine, at every moment of your life.
He whose mind is not perturbed by adversity, who does not crave prosperity, and who is free from fondness, fear, and anger, is a sage of constant wisdom.
Whatever a great man does, ordinary men follow.
It is far better to discharge one's prescribed duties, even though faultily, than another's duties perfectly.
Destruction awaits those who are controlled by greed, lust, and anger.
The knowledge of the self is the highest science.
The universe is an illusion, the self alone is real.
Wake up from the slumber of ignorance and realize your immortal nature.
He who controls his senses is indeed a conqueror.
The wise man does not grieve for the living or the dead.
All miseries arise from the ego; surrender the ego and be at peace.
When the mind is utterly quieted, the light of the self shines in all its glory.
Your true nature is absolute bliss.
The divine dwells in the lotus of the heart.
Meditate on the Omkara, the sound of the universe, to attain supreme peace.
The whole world is one single family.
Let good come to all, let peace prevail everywhere.
Contentment is the highest wealth.
He who is contented with whatever comes by chance, who has passed beyond the dualities, is free from envy, and is equable in success and failure, is not bound though he acts.
Sacrifice, charity, and penance are not to be given up; they must be performed.
When one is firmly established in non-violence, all beings around him cease to feel hostility.
When one is firmly established in truthfulness, one's actions bear the desired fruits.
When one is firmly established in non-stealing, all wealth comes to him.
When one is firmly established in continence, immense energy is gained.
When one is firmly established in non-possessiveness, knowledge of past, present, and future arises.
Through purity of mind comes cheerfulness, one-pointedness, control of the senses, and fitness for the vision of the Self.
From contentment arises supreme happiness.
Through austerity, impurities are destroyed and perfection in the body and senses is attained.
Through study of spiritual texts and introspection, communion with the presiding deity is established.
Through surrender to the Supreme Lord, the state of perfect concentration is attained.
The afflictions are ignorance, egoism, attachment, aversion, and clinging to life.
Ignorance is taking the non-eternal, impure, painful, and non-self for the eternal, pure, happy, and Atman (Self).
Pain that has not yet come is to be avoided.
The seer is pure consciousness, and though pure, he looks through the mind.
The purpose of the seen is only to serve the purpose of the seer.
By the practice of the limbs of Yoga, the impurities dwindle away and there dawns the light of wisdom.
Posture should be steady and comfortable.
Pranayama is the regulation of the incoming and outgoing flow of breath.
Concentration is the binding of the mind to one place or object.
Meditation is the continuous flow of perception or thought towards the object of concentration.
Samadhi is that state where the mind dissolves in the object of meditation and loses its own identity.
The ultimate goal of yoga is liberation, the isolation of the seer in his own pure nature.
See the divine play in all aspects of existence.
The supreme reality is an ocean of consciousness and bliss.
Sing the praises of the Lord, for it ends the miseries of life.
Tear the veil of delusion and behold the splendor of truth.
Life is a bridge, do not build a house upon it.
The world is a stage, and we are but actors playing our parts assigned by the divine.
The sun shines not there, nor the moon and stars, nor do these lightnings shine, much less this fire. When He shines, everything shines after Him.
Two ways lead to a human being, the good and the pleasant. The wise choose the good.
Words return along with the mind without attaining It. He who knows the bliss of Brahman fears not from anything at all.
This Self is unattainable by the weak, by the errant, or by those who do not practice austerity.
The bow is the syllable Om, the arrow is the Self, and Brahman is the target. One must shoot with an undistracted mind.
Om is the bow, the soul is the arrow, and Brahman is called its target. It is to be hit by an unerring man.
To him who sees the Lord equally present everywhere, all beings become as his own self.
Those who see all beings in the Self and the Self in all beings, shrink from nothing.
What delusion, what sorrow is there for him who sees this unity?
Radiant, subtler than the subtle, in it all the worlds and their inhabitants are established.
The wise who realize the eternal, knowing that the eternal cannot be gained by the non-eternal, do not seek the imperishable amidst the perishable.
The Self is not to be known by the study of the Vedas, nor by intellect, nor by much learning.
Awake, arise! Strive for the Highest, and be in the Light! Sages say the path is narrow and difficult to tread, narrow as the edge of a razor.

That which is without sound, without touch, without form, without decay, without taste, eternal, without smell, without beginning, without end, beyond the Great, and unchangeable—by knowing That, one is freed from the jaws of death.
The Creator pierced the senses outwards, hence one looks outward, not within. A rare discriminating man, desiring immortality, turns his eyes away and then sees the indwelling Self.
As fire, though one, takes the shape of every object it enters, so the inner Self of all beings, though one, takes the shape of every object it enters and is also outside them.
As air, though one, takes the shape of every object it enters, so the inner Self of all beings, though one, takes the shape of every object it enters and is also outside them.
He is the eternal among the non-eternal, the conscious among the conscious, the One among the many who fulfils desires.
There is only one Supreme Ruler, the inmost Self of all beings, who makes His one form manifold. Eternal happiness belongs to the wise, who perceive Him within themselves.
The sun does not shine there, nor the moon and the stars, nor these lightnings, and much less this fire. When that shines, everything shines after that. By its light all this is lighted.
When all the desires that dwell in the heart fall away, then the mortal becomes immortal and here attains Brahman.
When all the knots of the heart are severed here on earth, then the mortal becomes immortal. This much alone is the teaching.
A hundred and one are the nerves of the heart. Of them, one goes out through the head. Going up by it, one attains immortality.
The Purusha of the size of a thumb, the inner Self, is always seated in the heart of all beings. One should draw him out from one's own body with steadiness, as one draws the pith from a reed.

Beyond the senses are the objects; beyond the objects is the mind; beyond the mind, the intellect; beyond the intellect, the Great Atman; beyond the Great Atman, the Unmanifest; beyond the Unmanifest, the Purusha. Beyond the Purusha there is nothing.
The Self is hidden in all beings and does not shine forth, but it is seen by subtle seers through their one-pointed and subtle intellects.
Arise, awake, and learn by approaching the excellent ones.
Om, peace, peace, peace.
The infinite is bliss. There is no bliss in anything finite.
Where one sees nothing else, hears nothing else, understands nothing else, that is the Infinite.
Where one sees something else, hears something else, understands something else, that is the finite.
The Infinite is immortal, the finite is mortal.
Sir, in what does the Infinite rest? In its own greatness, or not even in greatness.
He who understands this, who reflects upon this, who perceives this, loves the Self, revels in the Self, rejoices in the Self, delights in the Self.

He who sees, thinks, and understands this, from him spring the vital breath, hope, memory, ether, heat, water, unmanifestation, manifestation, food, strength, understanding, meditation, thought, resolve, mind, speech, name, sacred hymns, and sacred works.
As rivers flowing down to the ocean lose their name and form and disappear into the ocean, so the wise man, freed from name and form, attains the Supreme Being, the Highest of the High.
Truth alone triumphs, not untruth. By truth is laid out the path of the gods.
The Lord of the Universe dwells in the hearts of all beings, turning them around like pieces of machinery.
The man who sees the Lord equally in all things, does not injure the Self by the self, and thus attains the highest goal.
He who offers to me with devotion only a leaf, or a flower, or a fruit, or even a little water, this I accept from that yearning soul, because with a pure heart it was offered with love.
Abandon all dharmas and take refuge in Me alone. I will deliver you from all sins; do not grieve.
He who has no enemies, who is friendly and compassionate, free from 'mine' and 'I', even-minded in pain and pleasure, and patient, that devotee is dear to Me.
He from whom the world does not shrink and who does not shrink from the world, free from exaltation, anger, fear, and agitation, he is dear to Me.
He who has no expectations, is pure, skillful, unconcerned, and untroubled, who has given up all initiative in action, he, My devotee, is dear to Me.
He who neither rejoices nor hates, neither grieves nor desires, who has renounced good and evil, full of devotion, he is dear to Me.
Alike to foe and friend, unchanged in honor and dishonor, the same in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, free from attachment.
To whom praise and blame are equal, who is silent, content with anything, homeless, steady-minded, full of devotion—that man is dear to Me.
They who follow this immortal dharma described above, endued with faith, holding Me as the supreme goal, and devoted—they are exceedingly dear to Me.
Therefore, always remember Me and fight. With mind and intellect surrendered to Me, you will undoubtedly attain Me.
With the mind made steadfast by Yoga, which turns not to anything else, meditating on the Supreme Resplendent Purusha, one goes to Him, O Partha.
Whosoever, at the time of death, leaves his body remembering Me alone, goes to My state; there is no doubt about it.
Thinking of whatever state of being he leaves his body at the end, to that he goes, O son of Kunti, being ever absorbed in the thought thereof.
He who remembers the Poet, the Ancient, the Ruler, the Subtlest of the subtle, the Supporter of all, whose form is inconceivable, resplendent like the sun and beyond darkness.
At the time of death, with an unmoving mind, endued with devotion and power of Yoga, fixing the Prana exactly between the eyebrows, he goes to the Supreme Resplendent Purusha.
That which the knowers of the Veda call Imperishable, into which the ascetics, freed from passion, enter, desiring which they practice Brahmacharya, that goal I will declare to thee briefly.
Closing all the gates of the body, confining the mind in the heart, fixing his Prana in the head, engaged in firm Yoga.
Uttering the one-syllabled Brahman, Om, and remembering Me, he who departs, leaving the body, attains the Supreme Goal.
He who constantly remembers Me, thinking of none else, of him who is a constantly engaged Yogi, I am easily reached, O Partha.
Having come to Me, these great souls do not get rebirth, which is the place of sorrow and is non-eternal, for they have reached the highest perfection.
All the worlds, including the realm of Brahma, are subject to return, O Arjuna; but he who reaches Me, O son of Kunti, has no rebirth.
Those who know the day of Brahma, which lasts a thousand Yugas, and the night, which also ends in a thousand Yugas, they know day and night.
From the unmanifest, all manifest things spring forth at the coming of the day; at the coming of the night, they merge in that same, called the unmanifest.
This multitude of beings, being born again and again, merges helplessly at the coming of the night, O Partha, and comes forth at the coming of the day.
But beyond this unmanifested, there is another Unmanifested, Eternal Being, who does not perish when all beings perish.
That Unmanifested is called the Imperishable; they call It the Supreme Goal. Those who attain It return not. That is My Supreme Abode.
That Supreme Purusha, O Partha, is attainable by unswerving devotion, in whom all beings dwell, and by whom all this is pervaded.
Now I will declare to thee the times departing at which the Yogis return or do not return, O prince of the Bharatas.
Fire, light, day, the bright fortnight, the six months of the northern path of the sun—departing then, the men who know Brahman go to Brahman.
Smoke, night, the dark fortnight, the six months of the southern path of the sun—departing then, the Yogi attains the lunar light and returns.
These two paths, bright and dark, are deemed to be the eternal paths of the world; by the one a man goes to not return, by the other he returns.
Knowing these two paths, O Partha, no Yogi is deluded; therefore, at all times be steadfast in Yoga, O Arjuna.
The Yogi who knows this transcends all the rewards of meritorious deeds prescribed in the Vedas, sacrifices, austerities, and charities, and attains the Supreme, Primal Abode.
The Supreme Lord said: To you who are free from fault I will now declare this most profound knowledge, together with realization, knowing which you will be freed from inauspiciousness.

This is the king of education, the most secret of all secrets. It is the purest knowledge, and because it gives direct perception of the self by realization, it is the perfection of religion. It is everlasting, and it is joyfully performed.
Men who are devoid of faith in this path of righteousness, O scorcher of foes, do not attain Me, but return to the path of the mortal world.
By Me, in My unmanifested form, this entire universe is pervaded. All beings are in Me, but I am not in them.
And yet beings are not in Me. Behold My cosmic mystery! My spirit, which is the source of all beings, sustains all beings but does not abide in them.
As the mighty wind, blowing everywhere, rests always in the ethereal space, know that in the same manner all beings rest in Me.
At the end of a Kalpa, all beings enter My lower nature, O son of Kunti; at the beginning of a Kalpa, I send them forth again.
Pressing My own nature into service, I send forth again and again this helpless multitude of beings by the power of nature.
And these actions do not bind Me, O Dhananjaya, sitting like one indifferent, unattached to these actions.
Under My guidance, nature produces the moving and the unmoving; by this means, O son of Kunti, the world revolves.
Fools disregard Me when clad in human form, not knowing My higher nature as the Great Lord of beings.
Vain are their hopes, vain their actions, vain their knowledge, and senseless; they possess the delusive nature of Rakshasas and Asuras.
But the great souls, O Partha, partaking of My divine nature, worship Me with a single mind, knowing Me to be the imperishable source of beings.
Always glorifying Me, striving, firm in vows, prostrating before Me with devotion, they worship Me, ever steadfast.
Others, too, sacrificing with the sacrifice of wisdom, worship Me as the One, as the distinct, and as the manifold, facing everywhere.
I am the ritual, I am the sacrifice, I am the offering, I am the healing herb, I am the mantra, I am the butter, I am the fire, and I am the act of offering.
I am the father of this world, the mother, the dispenser, the grandfather, the object of knowledge, the purifier, the Omkara, the Rig, Sama, and Yajur Vedas.
I am the goal, the supporter, the Lord, the witness, the abode, the refuge, the friend, the origin, the dissolution, the foundation, the treasure-house, and the imperishable seed.
I give heat; I withhold and send forth the rain; I am immortality and also death; I am being and non-being, O Arjuna.

The knowers of the three Vedas, who drink the Soma juice and are purified of sin, worship Me by sacrifices, praying for the way to heaven. Reaching the holy world of Indra, they enjoy the celestial pleasures of the gods in heaven.
Having enjoyed the spacious heaven-world, they enter the mortal world when their merit is exhausted; thus, conforming to the religion of the three Vedas, desiring desires, they obtain the transient.
Those men who worship Me alone, thinking of no other, to them, ever steadfast, I ensure the acquisition of what they lack and the preservation of what they have.
Even those devotees who, endued with faith, worship other gods, they also worship Me alone, O son of Kunti, though by the wrong method.
I am the enjoyer and the Lord of all sacrifices, but they do not know Me in reality; therefore, they fall.
The worshipers of the gods go to the gods; the worshipers of the ancestors go to the ancestors; those who worship the spirits go to the spirits; and My worshipers come to Me.
Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer in sacrifice, whatever you give, whatever austerity you practice, O son of Kunti, do that as an offering unto Me.
Thus you shall be freed from the bonds of actions, yielding good and evil fruits; with your mind firmly set on the Yoga of renunciation, you shall become free and come to Me.
I am the same to all beings; to Me there is none hateful or dear. But those who worship Me with devotion are in Me, and I am also in them.
Even if the most sinful worships Me with undivided devotion, he must be regarded as righteous, for he has rightly resolved.
Soon he becomes righteous and obtains eternal peace. O son of Kunti, know for certain that My devotee never perishes.
For those who take refuge in Me, O Partha, even though they be of low birth, women, Vaisyas, and Sudras, they also attain the Supreme Goal.
How much more then the holy Brahmanas and devoted royal saints! Having obtained this transient, joyless world, worship Me.
Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, sacrifice to Me, bow down to Me. Having thus disciplined yourself, and regarding Me as the Supreme Goal, you will come to Me.
Again, O mighty-armed, hear My supreme word, which, desiring your welfare, I will speak to you who are beloved.
Neither the hosts of gods nor the great sages know My origin, for I am in every way the source of the gods and the great sages.
He who knows Me as unborn and beginningless, the Great Lord of the worlds, he among mortals is undeluded and freed from all sins.
Intellect, knowledge, non-delusion, forbearance, truth, control of the senses, control of the mind, pleasure, pain, birth, death, fear, and fearlessness.
Non-injury, equanimity, contentment, austerity, charity, fame, and ill fame—these different qualities of beings arise from Me alone.
The seven great sages and the four Manus of old, of whose lineage are all these creatures in the world, were born of My mind, possessing My power.
He who knows in truth this manifold manifestation and My Yoga power is united with Me by unfaltering Yoga; of this there is no doubt.
I am the source of all; from Me everything evolves. Understanding thus, the wise worship Me with all their heart.
With their minds completely fixed on Me, their lives surrendered to Me, enlightening one another and always speaking of Me, they find satisfaction and delight.
To those who are ever steadfast and worship Me with love, I give the Yoga of Discrimination, by which they come to Me.
Out of pure compassion for them, I, dwelling in their hearts, destroy the darkness born of ignorance with the luminous lamp of knowledge.
You are the Supreme Brahman, the Supreme Abode, the Supreme Purifier, the Eternal Divine Purusha, the Primal God, Unborn and Omnipresent.
All the sages have thus acclaimed You, as also the divine sage Narada, Asita, Devala, and Vyasa; and now You Yourself declare it to me.
I believe all this that You say to me to be true, O Keshava. Neither the gods nor the demons, O Lord, know Your manifestation.
You Yourself alone know Yourself by Yourself, O Supreme Purusha, O Source of beings, O Lord of beings, O God of gods, O Ruler of the world.
You should indeed speak in detail of Your divine manifestations, by which manifestations You exist pervading these worlds.
How shall I, ever meditating, know You, O Yogi? In what particular aspects are You to be thought of by me, O Blessed Lord?
Tell me again in detail, O Janardana, of Your Yoga power and Your manifestations; for I am not satiated with hearing Your nectar-like words.
The Blessed Lord said: Very well, I will declare to you My divine manifestations, but only the prominent ones, O best of the Kurus, for there is no end to My extent.
I am the Self, O Gudakesha, seated in the hearts of all beings. I am the beginning, the middle, and also the end of all beings.
Of the Adityas, I am Vishnu; of luminaries, the radiant sun; I am Marichi of the Maruts; of the stars, I am the moon.
Of the Vedas, I am the Sama Veda; of the gods, I am Indra; of the senses, I am the mind; and in living beings, I am consciousness.
Of the Rudras, I am Lord Shiva; of the Yakshas and Rakshasas, the Lord of wealth (Kubera); of the Vasus, I am Agni (fire); of mountains, I am Meru.
Know Me, O Partha, to be the chief among priests, Brihaspati. Of generals, I am Skanda; of bodies of water, I am the ocean.
Of the great sages, I am Bhrigu; of words, I am the one-syllabled Om; of sacrifices, I am the sacrifice of japa (silent repetition); of immovable things, the Himalayas.
Of all trees, I am the holy fig tree; of divine sages, Narada; of the Gandharvas, Chitraratha; of the perfected ones, the sage Kapila.
Know Me among horses as Uchchhaishravas, born of nectar; among lordly elephants, the Airavata; and among men, the king.
Of weapons, I am the thunderbolt; of cows, I am the wish-fulfilling cow, Kamadhenu; of progenitors, I am the god of love, Kandarpa; of serpents, I am Vasuki.
Of the Nagas, I am Ananta; of water deities, I am Varuna; of the ancestors, I am Aryaman; of controllers, I am Yama, the lord of death.
Of the Daityas, I am Prahlada; of calculators, I am time; of beasts, I am the lion; and of birds, I am Garuda.
Of purifiers, I am the wind; of warriors, I am Rama; of fishes, I am the shark; of rivers, I am the Ganges.
Of creations, I am the beginning, the end, and also the middle, O Arjuna; of sciences, the science of the Self; of those who debate, I am the dialectic.
Of letters, I am the letter A; of compounds, I am the dual; I am also inexhaustible time; I am the Dispenser, facing everywhere.
I am the all-devouring death, and the origin of things yet to be. Of feminine qualities, I am fame, prosperity, speech, memory, intelligence, firmness, and forgiveness.
Of the hymns of the Sama Veda, I am the Brihat-saman; of meters, I am the Gayatri; of months, I am Margashirsha; of seasons, the flowery spring.
I am the gambling of the deceitful, the splendor of the splendid; I am victory, I am effort, I am the goodness of the good.
Of the Vrishnis, I am Vasudeva (Krishna); of the Pandavas, I am Dhananjaya (Arjuna); of the sages, I am Vyasa; of the poets, I am Ushana the poet.
Of punishers, I am the scepter; of those who seek victory, I am statesmanship; of secrets, I am silence; of the knowers, I am the knowledge.
And whatever is the seed of all beings, that also am I, O Arjuna. There is no being, whether moving or unmoving, that can exist without Me.
There is no end to My divine manifestations, O scorcher of foes; what has been declared by Me is only an illustrative description of My infinite glory.
Whatever being is glorious, prosperous, or powerful, know that to be a manifestation of a part of My splendor.
But what need is there, O Arjuna, for this detailed knowledge? I stand supporting this entire universe with a single fragment of Myself.
By Your grace, O Lord, my delusion has been destroyed, and I have regained my memory. I remain firm, free from doubt, and will do Your bidding.
Wherever is Krishna, the Lord of Yoga, and wherever is Arjuna, the wielder of the bow, there will surely be fortune, victory, prosperity, and statecraft.
All the wealth of the world cannot help one little Indian village if the people are not taught to help themselves.
If you want to find God, serve man. To reach Narayana you must serve the Daridra Narayanas.
Truth does not pay homage to any society, ancient or modern. Society has to pay homage to Truth or die.
In the practice of non-violence, the bravest are those who never retaliate.
My religion is based on truth and non-violence. Truth is my God. Non-violence is the means of realizing Him.
The best way to find yourself is to lose yourself in the service of others.
Be the change that you wish to see in the world.
Peace comes from within. Do not seek it without.
Source: Buddha (Adopted into broader Indian spiritual context)

Quote: Health is the greatest gift, contentment the greatest wealth, faithfulness the best relationship.
The mind is everything. What you think you become.
No one saves us but ourselves. No one can and no one may. We ourselves must walk the path.
Doubt everything. Find your own light.
There are only two mistakes one can make along the road to truth; not going all the way, and not starting.
It is better to conquer yourself than to win a thousand battles.
To keep the body in good health is a duty... otherwise we shall not be able to keep our mind strong and clear.
You cannot travel the path until you have become the path itself.
A jug fills drop by drop.
In the end, only three things matter: how much you loved, how gently you lived, and how gracefully you let go of things not meant for you.
Radiate boundless love towards the entire world.
Even as a solid rock is unshaken by the wind, so are the wise unshaken by praise or blame.
Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world; by non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is a law eternal.
Those who are continually awake, who train themselves day and night, who are wholly intent on the deathless – their defilements come to an end.
By oneself is evil done; by oneself is one defiled. By oneself is evil left undone; by oneself is one made pure.
Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.
Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth.
The root of suffering is attachment.
Work out your own salvation. Do not depend on others.
Silence the angry man with love. Silence the ill-natured man with kindness. Silence the miser with generosity. Silence the liar with truth.
The only real failure in life is not to be true to the best one knows.
Better than a thousand hollow words, is one word that brings peace.
Believe nothing, no matter where you read it, or who said it, no matter if I have said it, unless it agrees with your own reason and your own common sense.
You will not be punished for your anger, you will be punished by your anger.
Everything that has a beginning has an ending. Make your peace with that and all will be well.
An insincere and evil friend is more to be feared than a wild beast.
The way is not in the sky. The way is in the heart.
Just as a candle cannot burn without fire, men cannot live without a spiritual life.
Peace is the highest bliss.
To abstain from all evil, to cultivate the good, and to purify one's mind—this is the teaching of the awakened.
Mind goes before all mental states. Mind is their chief, they are all mind-wrought.
He who experiences the unity of life sees his own Self in all beings.
Do not dwell in the past, do not dream of the future, concentrate the mind on the present moment.
Three things cannot be long hidden: the sun, the moon, and the truth.
The mind is restless and difficult to restrain, but it is subdued by practice.
Happiness never decreases by being shared.
Know the Atman as the Lord of the Chariot, the body as the chariot itself, the intellect as the charioteer, and the mind as the reins.
The senses, they say, are the horses, and the objects of the senses are their paths.
When the Atman is united with the body, the senses, and the mind, the wise call It the enjoyer.
He who has no understanding and whose mind is never firmly held, his senses are unmanageable, like vicious horses of a charioteer.
He who has understanding and whose mind is always firmly held, his senses are under control, like good horses of a charioteer.
He who has no understanding, who is unmindful and always impure, never reaches that goal, but enters into the round of births.
He who has understanding, who is mindful and always pure, reaches indeed that goal from which he is not born again.
He who has understanding for his charioteer, and who holds the reins of the mind, he reaches the end of his journey, the supreme abode of the all-pervading Vishnu.
